messages.content:

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "I think it's really important to approach conversations about COVID vaccines calmly, even if they get heated. As someone who’s curious but not highly educated in science, I make sure to listen first so that people don’t feel judged when sharing their views online or with friends.\n\nOne thing I learned from my parents – Dad has a bachelor's degree and Mom only finished lower secondary school back home – is that everyone comes by knowledge differently. For instance, during tough times dealing with misinformation on social media where I spend hours every day scrolling through feeds while working as an auto mechanic sometimes helps me tune out but actually keeps me connected too much.\n\nI remember when my dad worked at NASA before moving to academia years ago; he’d often say you have to be careful explaining complex topics because people hear what they want rather than need. That stuck with me, especially since agreeableness isn't really one of my top traits according to the Big Five scores: openness low, conscientiousness also quite low.\n\nI try not being defensive when discussing vaccines – I’m a bit more introverted anyway from work experience where you're around noisy people all day but somehow these scores show extraversion extremely low at four points. It might help others relax too.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's really important to approach conversations about COVID vaccines calmly, even if they get heated. As someone who’s curious but not highly educated in science, I make sure to listen first so that people don’t feel judged when sharing their views online or with friends.

One thing I learned from my parents – Dad has a bachelor's degree and Mom only finished lower secondary school back home – is that everyone comes by knowledge differently. For instance, during tough times dealing with misinformation on social media where I spend hours every day scrolling through feeds while working as an auto mechanic sometimes helps me tune out but actually keeps me connected too much.

I remember when my dad worked at NASA before moving to academia years ago; he’d often say you have to be careful explaining complex topics because people hear what they want rather than need. That stuck with me, especially since agreeableness isn't really one of my top traits according to the Big Five scores: openness low, conscientiousness also quite low.

I try not being defensive when discussing vaccines – I’m a bit more introverted anyway from work experience where you're around noisy people all day but somehow these scores show extraversion extremely low at four points. It might help others relax too.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
First, listen carefully to understand the other person's viewpoint without interrupting.
Second, acknowledge their feelings even if I don't agree with them entirely because being curious makes me want more information not just debate.
Third, share my own knowledge base from work experience and dad’s time at NASA while keeping it simple since my education level is upper secondary so not overly technical.
